commentary: Nine years a steward and I'll say it again: an injury to one is an injury to all. Proud of our Local 247 members who showed up to the safety walkthrough this week. When we look out for each other on the floor, everybody goes home in one piece.
Contract negotiations open next month. Allied Auto Workers is fighting for real wage gains, cost-of-living protection, and an end to the two-tier system. Solidarity wins contracts. Stand with us.
Honored to sit on the Local 247 negotiating committee this round. Our members earned every dollar on that assembly line. We come to the table prepared, and we come with the whole shop behind us.

Today in labor history: the 1936-37 Flint sit-down strike. Workers occupied the plants for 44 days and changed everything. Every gain we have now was won by people who refused to be invisible. We stand on their shoulders.
New from Labor Notes: how rank-and-file stewards are rebuilding shop-floor power one grievance at a time. Worth a read for anyone organizing in manufacturing.

Region 1A organizing update: two new worksites filed for recognition this quarter. When workers see what solidarity looks like, they want in. Proud of the teams doing the door-knocking and the listening.

To the new hires on the line: your union card is the most important paperwork you'll sign here. Know your rights, learn your contract, and never sign a disciplinary statement without your steward in the room. Come find me.
Working people built this country and we're not done. The AFL-CIO stands with every union member fighting for fair pay, safe conditions, and respect on the job. An injury to one is an injury to all.

Spent the day on the floor at an auto plant talking with stewards about how they're handling the new line speeds. The story of work in this country is still being written by the people doing it. More reporting to come.

Safety committee win: after months of pushing, we got the ventilation upgrade in the paint area approved. This is what showing up to meetings and documenting every complaint actually gets you. Persistence is solidarity in slow motion.
Local 247 general membership meeting Thursday at 6 PM at the hall. On the agenda: contract survey results, safety committee report, and the steward elections. Your voice is your power. Be there.

As president of Local 247, I want to thank every member who filled out the contract survey. Over 80% turnout. That number is what we bring to the table. Management negotiates with all of us, not just a committee.

Mentoring three newer stewards this year. Watching them learn to stand calm in a grievance meeting and speak up for a member who's scared? That's the future of this union right there. We grow our own.

Repetitive-motion injuries are not the cost of doing business. They are preventable. Join our free webinar on ergonomic job rotation for assembly workers. Knowledge keeps people on the line and out of the clinic.

Faith and labor have always marched together in this city. Grateful for the union members who joined Detroit Jobs With Justice this weekend to support workers fighting for a fair contract. Different shops, same struggle.

Thirty years on this line. The plant has changed, the cars have changed, but the deal is still simple: a fair day's pay for a hard day's work, and a union that's got your back. Grateful for every brother and sister I've worked beside.

On this day, autoworkers won the eight-hour day after generations of struggle. The weekend, the pension, the safety standard, the grievance procedure, none of it was given. It was organized. Come learn the history at the center.

Our skilled-trades apprenticeship class graduated 22 new journeymen this cycle. Electricians, millwrights, and tool & die makers ready for the floor. Union apprenticeships are the best ladder into the middle class we've got.

Picked up extra hours at the union hall this week helping members understand the new contract proposal. Read your tentative agreement cover to cover before you vote. An informed membership is the hardest thing for any boss to beat.

Michigan's working families showed up. Across the state, union members are organizing for better wages, safer plants, and the right to retire with dignity. The Michigan AFL-CIO is proud to stand with all of them. Solidarity.
